enrichment.short_description: Suricata is a free, open-source network intrusion detection (IDS), intrusion prevention (IPS), and network security monitoring (NSM) engine maintained by the Open Information Security Foundation (OISF).
enrichment.seo_title: Suricata: Open-Source Network IDS, IPS and NSM Engine
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Suricata is a free, open-source network threat detection engine offering IDS, IPS, and NSM. Built and maintained by the nonprofit OISF community.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Suricata is a free, open-source network intrusion detection system (IDS), intrusion prevention system (IPS), and network security monitoring (NSM) engine. It is developed and maintained by the Open Information Security Foundation (OISF), a nonprofit organization that began work on the project around 2007 and released the first public beta at the end of 2009, with initial funding support from the US Department of Homeland Security and a consortium of private security vendors. Suricata is used by enterprises, government agencies, managed security service providers, and commercial security vendors who embed it into their own products. It is multi-threaded for high-performance packet processing, supports deep packet inspection, protocol detection, Lua scripting, JSON (EVE) event output, TLS and JA3 fingerprinting, and file extraction. It is compatible with Snort-style rules and works with community rule sets such as Emerging Threats Open. Suricata is entirely free to download and self-host, released as open source under the GNU General Public License, with no official paid tier from OISF itself, though third-party vendors like Stamus Networks offer commercial support and management platforms built around it. It is best suited for security teams with the networking expertise to deploy and tune an inline or passive detection engine.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Suricata
paragraphs: Suricata is a high-performance, open-source network analysis and threat detection engine that functions as an intrusion detection system (IDS), intrusion prevention system (IPS), and network security monitoring (NSM) tool. It inspects network traffic in real time, matching packets against rule sets to flag or block malicious activity.

The project is developed and governed by the Open Information Security Foundation (OISF), a 501(c)(3) nonprofit created to build a next-generation, vendor-neutral IDS/IPS engine as an alternative to existing tools. Development began in the mid-to-late 2000s under founders including Matt Jonkman and Victor Julien, with the first public beta released at the end of 2009.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Suricata's multi-threaded architecture allows it to process high volumes of network traffic more efficiently than older single-threaded engines. It supports deep packet inspection across dozens of protocols, automatic protocol detection regardless of port, and a Lua scripting interface for custom detection logic.

Output is available in structured JSON (EVE) format, making it easy to feed into SIEM platforms and tools like Elasticsearch or Security Onion. Suricata also performs file extraction, TLS and JA3/JA3S fingerprinting, and supports hardware acceleration through AF_PACKET, DPDK, and eBPF/XDP for high-throughput deployments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Suricata is completely free and open source under the GNU General Public License. There is no paid tier, subscription, or license fee from OISF for using the core engine.

Organizations that want commercial support, managed rule feeds, or a bundled management console typically turn to third-party vendors such as Stamus Networks, which build commercial products and services around the open-source Suricata engine.

features.title: Multi-threaded detection engine
features.description: Processes network traffic across multiple CPU cores for higher throughput than legacy single-threaded IDS engines.

features.title: IDS, IPS, and NSM modes
features.description: Can run passively for detection and monitoring or inline as an intrusion prevention system that actively blocks traffic.

features.title: Deep packet inspection
features.description: Inspects traffic across many protocols with automatic protocol detection independent of the port used.

features.title: JSON (EVE) logging
features.description: Outputs structured event logs that integrate with SIEMs, Elasticsearch, and dashboards like EveBox.

features.title: Snort-compatible rule language
features.description: Works with existing Snort-style rules and community rule sets such as Emerging Threats Open.

features.title: TLS and JA3 fingerprinting
features.description: Identifies encrypted traffic characteristics and client/server fingerprints without decrypting payloads.

features.title: Lua scripting
features.description: Allows custom detection logic beyond standard rule syntax for advanced use cases.

features.title: Hardware acceleration support
features.description: Supports AF_PACKET, DPDK, and eBPF/XDP for high-throughput packet capture and processing.

pros: Completely free and open source with no licensing cost
High-performance multi-threaded engine suited for large networks
Vendor-neutral governance backed by a nonprofit foundation and industry consortium
Flexible deployment as IDS, IPS, or NSM
Broad ecosystem of compatible rule sets and third-party tooling
cons: Steep learning curve for writing and tuning detection rules
Requires strong networking and security expertise to deploy at scale
No built-in graphical management console out of the box
No official commercial support directly from OISF
Effective use depends heavily on quality of external rule feeds

faqs.question: Is Suricata free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Suricata is fully open source under the GNU General Public License and free to download, deploy, and use without licensing fees.

faqs.question: What is Suricata used for?
faqs.answer: Suricata is used for network intrusion detection, intrusion prevention, and network security monitoring, helping security teams identify and block malicious network activity.

faqs.question: Who maintains Suricata?
faqs.answer: Suricata is developed and maintained by the Open Information Security Foundation (OISF), a nonprofit organization, along with an open community of contributors.

faqs.question: How is Suricata different from Snort?
faqs.answer: Suricata was built as a multi-threaded alternative to Snort, offering higher performance on multi-core hardware while remaining compatible with Snort-style rules.

faqs.question: Can Suricata run inline as an IPS?
faqs.answer: Yes, Suricata can be deployed inline to actively block malicious traffic in addition to running passively as a detection and monitoring tool.

faqs.question: Does Suricata include a management dashboard?
faqs.answer: No built-in GUI is included, but Suricata integrates with third-party tools like EveBox, Security Onion, and SELKS for visualization and management.

faqs.question: What rule sets work with Suricata?
faqs.answer: Suricata supports Snort-compatible rules and is commonly paired with community rule feeds such as Emerging Threats Open, plus commercial feeds from vendors.

faqs.question: Is commercial support available for Suricata?
faqs.answer: OISF itself does not sell commercial support, but third-party vendors such as Stamus Networks offer commercial products and support built on Suricata.

use_cases.title: Enterprise network security monitoring
use_cases.description: Security teams deploy Suricata to continuously inspect network traffic and detect threats across corporate networks.

use_cases.title: Managed security service delivery
use_cases.description: MSSPs embed Suricata into their monitoring stack to deliver threat detection services to multiple clients.

use_cases.title: Critical infrastructure and government threat detection
use_cases.description: Government agencies and critical infrastructure operators use Suricata for vendor-neutral, auditable network threat detection.
use_cases.audience: Government and critical infrastructure security teams

features: Full IDS, IPS, and NSM engine
Compatible with community rule sets like Emerging Threats Open

enrichment.short_description: Suroi is a free, open-source 2D battle royale game that runs directly in the browser, inspired by surviv.io.
enrichment.seo_title: Suroi: Free Open-Source 2D Battle Royale Browser Game
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Suroi is a free, open-source 2D battle royale game playable instantly in your browser, inspired by surviv.io. No download required.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Suroi is a free, open-source 2D battle royale game that players can access instantly through a web browser without downloading or installing anything. It is developed by HasangerGames, an open-source game development team, with the project's codebase first appearing publicly around 2023. Suroi is explicitly inspired by the earlier browser battle royale game surviv.io and recreates a similar top-down, last-player-standing gameplay loop featuring a shrinking safe zone, a variety of firearms and melee weapons, loot collection, and real-time player statistics such as kills and survival streaks. The game regularly ships limited-time seasonal content, including Halloween and winter-themed events with unique mechanics like ice physics, along with alternate modes such as sniper-only spawns. Suroi's source code is publicly available on GitHub under an open-source license, and the project supports self-hosting for communities that want to run private servers. There is no cost to play, no premium currency, and no company behind it in the traditional sense — it is a community-maintained project rather than a commercial SaaS product. It is best suited for casual browser gamers seeking quick matches and for developers curious about studying or contributing to an open-source multiplayer game engine.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Suroi
paragraphs: Suroi is a free, open-source 2D battle royale game that runs entirely in a web browser. Players drop into a shrinking map, scavenge weapons and gear, and fight to be the last one standing, in a style directly inspired by the earlier browser hit surviv.io.

The game is built and maintained by HasangerGames, with active development visible on GitHub. Because it is open source, anyone can inspect the code, contribute changes, or self-host their own server instance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Suroi offers classic battle royale mechanics: a shrinking safe zone, varied loot including sniper rifles, bows, and melee weapons, and real-time tracking of kills and survival streaks. The interface includes a minimap, gas timer, and configurable HUD and graphics settings ranging from low-end to 1080p.

The developers regularly release limited-time seasonal events, such as Halloween and winter modes with special mechanics like ice physics, as well as alternate spawn and squad configurations to keep matches fresh.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Suroi is entirely free to play with no purchase required, no premium currency, and no paywalled content on the official site.
Because the source code is open, technically inclined users can self-host their own instance at no cost beyond their own server hosting expenses.

features.title: Browser-based gameplay
features.description: Play instantly at suroi.io with no download, install, or account setup required.

features.title: Top-down 2D battle royale
features.description: Classic last-player-standing gameplay with a shrinking safe zone and loot-based progression.

features.title: Varied weapon loadouts
features.description: Includes sniper rifles, bows, shotguns, and melee weapons for different playstyles.

features.title: Seasonal limited-time events
features.description: Regularly rotating Halloween, winter, and other themed modes with unique mechanics.

features.title: Open-source codebase
features.description: Full source code is publicly available on GitHub for inspection and contribution.

features.title: Self-hosting support
features.description: Communities can run their own private Suroi server instance.

features.title: Customizable HUD and graphics
features.description: Adjustable graphics quality, crosshairs, emotes, and performance display options.

features.title: Active community wiki
features.description: A dedicated wiki documents weapons, mechanics, and updates maintained by the community.

pros: Completely free with no purchases or premium currency
No download or installation needed to start playing
Open-source and self-hostable for private communities
Actively updated with new seasonal content
Lightweight enough to run in most modern browsers
cons: Still labeled a work in progress with occasional bugs
Smaller player base than major commercial battle royale titles
No official monetization means limited dedicated support resources
Performance can vary depending on device and browser
Feature set and stability can change quickly between updates

faqs.question: Is Suroi free to play?
faqs.answer: Yes, Suroi is completely free with no purchases, subscriptions, or premium currency.

faqs.question: Do I need to download anything to play Suroi?
faqs.answer: No, Suroi runs directly in a web browser at suroi.io with no installation required.

faqs.question: Who created Suroi?
faqs.answer: Suroi is developed by HasangerGames, an open-source game development team, with its source code hosted on GitHub.

faqs.question: Is Suroi open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, Suroi's full source code is publicly available and open to community contributions.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Suroi?
faqs.answer: Yes, because the code is open source, technically capable users can run their own private server instance.

faqs.question: What inspired Suroi?
faqs.answer: Suroi is explicitly inspired by the earlier browser battle royale game surviv.io.

faqs.question: Is there mobile support for Suroi?
faqs.answer: The project maintains related repositories exploring iOS and Android access in addition to the core browser game.

faqs.question: Is Suroi actively updated?
faqs.answer: Yes, the game receives regular updates including seasonal events and new content.

enrichment.short_description: SurrealDB is a multi-model database combining document, graph, relational, and vector data in a single engine, usable embedded, self-hosted, or via SurrealDB Cloud.
enrichment.seo_title: SurrealDB: Multi-Model Database for Modern Applications
enrichment.seo_meta_description: SurrealDB is a multi-model database with document, graph, relational, and vector support. Free to self-host or use SurrealDB Cloud from $0.021/hr.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: SurrealDB is a multi-model database that unifies document, graph, relational, vector, and time-series data handling within a single engine, aiming to replace the need to combine several specialized databases in one application stack. It was founded in 2021 by brothers Tobie Morgan Hitchcock (CEO) and Jaime Morgan Hitchcock (COO), and is headquartered in London, United Kingdom, with a team of roughly 45 people as of 2025. The company has raised over 40 million dollars in venture funding, including a Series A extension backed by investors such as FirstMark, Georgian, Chalfen Ventures, and Begin Capital. SurrealDB uses its own query language, SurrealQL, supports real-time live queries, ACID transactions, built-in authentication and permissions, and can run embedded in an application, self-hosted on a server, or through the managed SurrealDB Cloud service. It targets serverless, JAMstack, and AI-driven applications that need vector search alongside traditional relational or graph queries. The core database is source-available and free to self-host, while SurrealDB Cloud uses usage-based pricing starting with a free instance and scaling to per-node hourly rates, plus a custom-priced Enterprise tier for self-hosted or private-cloud deployments with SSO, audit logging, and SLA guarantees.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is SurrealDB
paragraphs: SurrealDB is a multi-model database designed to replace the common pattern of stitching together separate document, graph, relational, and vector databases within a single application. It provides document, graph, relational, and vector capabilities through one unified engine and query language called SurrealQL.

Founded in 2021 by brothers Tobie and Jaime Morgan Hitchcock and headquartered in London, SurrealDB has raised over 40 million dollars from investors including FirstMark and Georgian, and offers the database as an embeddable engine, a self-hosted server, or a managed cloud service called SurrealDB Cloud.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: SurrealDB supports real-time live queries that push updates to connected clients as data changes, along with ACID-compliant transactions, built-in row-level authentication and permissions, and native vector search for AI and retrieval-augmented applications.

It can be embedded directly into an application as a single binary or WebAssembly module, deployed self-hosted, or run through SurrealDB Cloud, which offers vertical scaling on its Start tier and horizontal, multi-availability-zone scaling on its Scale tier. Some advanced cloud capabilities, such as database branching and forking, are planned for a future release.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: SurrealDB can be self-hosted for free using its source-available core. SurrealDB Cloud offers a Start plan with one free instance and 1GB of free storage, then usage-based pricing beginning around $0.021 per hour.

The Scale plan is priced per node per hour, starting around $0.192, and adds multi-availability-zone deployment and multi-region disaster recovery. An Enterprise tier offers custom pricing for self-hosted or private-cloud deployments with SSO, audit logging, and SLA-backed uptime.

features.title: Multi-model data engine
features.description: Combines document, graph, relational, vector, and time-series capabilities in one database.

features.title: SurrealQL query language
features.description: A purpose-built query language designed to express complex multi-model queries.

features.title: Real-time live queries
features.description: Pushes live updates to connected clients as underlying data changes.

features.title: Built-in auth and permissions
features.description: Row-level authentication and access control are handled natively in the database.

features.title: Flexible deployment
features.description: Runs embedded in an app, self-hosted on a server, or as a managed SurrealDB Cloud instance.

features.title: Native vector search
features.description: Supports vector data types and search for AI and retrieval-augmented generation use cases.

features.title: ACID transactions
features.description: Provides transactional consistency guarantees across multi-model operations.

features.title: Horizontal and vertical scaling
features.description: SurrealDB Cloud scales from a single free instance up to multi-region, multi-node clusters.

pros: Replaces multiple specialized databases with a single multi-model engine
Flexible deployment options from embedded to fully managed cloud
Free tier and free self-hosted use for prototyping and small projects
Well-funded with backing from established venture investors
Native vector search suited to modern AI application needs
cons: Newer database with a shorter production track record than established systems
Some advanced cloud features like branching and forking are still rolling out
SurrealQL requires learning a new query language distinct from standard SQL
Source-available licensing terms may require review for some enterprise use cases
Smaller ecosystem of third-party tools and integrations compared to legacy databases

faqs.question: Is SurrealDB free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core database can be self-hosted for free, and SurrealDB Cloud offers a free starting instance with usage-based pricing beyond that.

faqs.question: What is SurrealDB used for?
faqs.answer: SurrealDB is used to store and query document, graph, relational, and vector data within a single database, commonly for serverless, JAMstack, and AI-powered applications.

faqs.question: Who founded SurrealDB?
faqs.answer: SurrealDB was founded in 2021 by brothers Tobie Morgan Hitchcock and Jaime Morgan Hitchcock.

faqs.question: Where is SurrealDB headquartered?
faqs.answer: SurrealDB is headquartered in London, United Kingdom.

faqs.question: Can SurrealDB replace Postgres and MongoDB together?
faqs.answer: SurrealDB aims to cover relational and document use cases in one engine, though teams should evaluate it against their specific workload requirements before migrating.

faqs.question: Does SurrealDB support vector search for AI?
faqs.answer: Yes, SurrealDB includes native vector data types and search capabilities suited to AI and retrieval-augmented generation applications.

faqs.question: Can I self-host SurrealDB?
faqs.answer: Yes, SurrealDB can be self-hosted on your own infrastructure at no licensing cost for the core engine.

faqs.question: What is SurrealQL?
faqs.answer: SurrealQL is SurrealDB's purpose-built query language designed to express document, graph, relational, and vector queries.

use_cases.title: Unified data layer for serverless apps
use_cases.description: Startups use SurrealDB as a single database for document, relational, and graph data in serverless and JAMstack applications.

use_cases.title: AI and retrieval-augmented generation
use_cases.description: AI teams combine vector search with relational and graph data in one engine for RAG and semantic search pipelines.

use_cases.title: Database consolidation for enterprises
use_cases.description: Enterprises use SurrealDB to reduce the number of specialized databases they operate and maintain.

enrichment.short_description: SurveyJS is an open-source JavaScript library suite that lets developers build and embed custom surveys, forms, and dashboards directly inside their own web applications.
enrichment.seo_title: SurveyJS: Open-Source JavaScript Form and Survey Library
enrichment.seo_meta_description: SurveyJS is an open-source JS library for embedding custom surveys and forms in your own app. Free Form Library, paid Creator from $569 one-time.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: SurveyJS is an open-source suite of JavaScript libraries that developers use to build, render, and embed custom surveys, forms, and dashboards inside their own web applications, rather than hosting surveys on a third-party site. It is developed by Devsoft Baltic OU, a small Estonian software company founded in 2015 and headquartered in Tallinn, Estonia, with a lean team of roughly two to ten people. SurveyJS is distinct from hosted survey products like SurveyMonkey: it is a developer tool that renders forms defined as JSON directly inside a company's own application, so all response data stays on the company's own servers rather than a third-party platform. The core Form Library is MIT-licensed and free to use with unlimited forms, submissions, and file uploads. Paid, one-time perpetual developer licenses unlock additional tools: the Basic tier (starting around 569 US dollars) adds the drag-and-drop Survey Creator, the PRO tier (around 1,029 US dollars) adds a Dashboard and PDF Generator, and a custom-priced Enterprise tier adds dedicated technical support. Licenses include 12 months of updates and support, with optional annual renewal afterward, and are backed by a 90-day money-back guarantee. It works with React, Angular, Vue, and vanilla JavaScript.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is SurveyJS
paragraphs: SurveyJS is an open-source JavaScript library suite for developers who need to add custom surveys, forms, or dashboards directly inside their own applications, rather than sending users to a third-party hosted survey site. Forms are defined as JSON and rendered by SurveyJS's MIT-licensed Form Library.

It is built and maintained by Devsoft Baltic OU, a small software company founded in 2015 and based in Tallinn, Estonia. Because SurveyJS runs inside the developer's own application, all response data is stored on the developer's own infrastructure rather than a third-party server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The free, MIT-licensed Form Library renders JSON-defined surveys and forms in any JavaScript application, with support for React, Angular, Vue, and vanilla JS. It includes extensive question types, conditional logic, and branching.

Paid add-ons extend the free library: Survey Creator is a drag-and-drop visual form builder, the Dashboard library provides interactive data visualization and reporting, and the PDF Generator exports completed forms and results to PDF, Excel, or CSV.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Essential tier is free and includes the open-source Form Library with unlimited forms, submissions, and file uploads, since data is hosted on the developer's own servers.

Paid tiers are one-time perpetual developer licenses rather than recurring subscriptions: Basic starts around 569 US dollars and adds Survey Creator, PRO starts around 1,029 US dollars and adds the Dashboard and PDF Generator, and Enterprise starts around 2,299 US dollars with dedicated support. Each license includes 12 months of updates, with optional annual renewal afterward and a 90-day money-back guarantee.

features.title: Drag-and-drop Survey Creator

features.description: Exports forms and results to PDF, Excel, or CSV.

features.title: Self-hosted data ownership
features.description: Response data stays on the developer's own servers rather than a third-party host.

features.title: Conditional logic and branching
features.description: Supports skip logic, dynamic question visibility, and complex form flows.

features.title: Perpetual one-time licensing
features.description: Paid tiers are one-time developer license purchases rather than forced recurring subscriptions.

Full data ownership since forms run inside the developer's own app

Highly customizable question types and logic
cons: Requires development resources to implement, unlike no-code survey tools
Commercial tiers require upfront payment for the Creator, Dashboard, and PDF Generator
Small company size means a slower feature release pace than large SaaS vendors
Documentation is developer-oriented and less accessible to non-technical users
No built-in survey distribution or audience/panel features like hosted competitors

faqs.question: Is SurveyJS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core Form Library is free and open source under the MIT license. Paid one-time licenses unlock the Survey Creator, Dashboard, and PDF Generator tools.

faqs.question: What is the difference between SurveyJS and SurveyMonkey?
faqs.answer: SurveyJS is an open-source developer library you embed and self-host inside your own application, while SurveyMonkey is a hosted SaaS platform where surveys and data live on SurveyMonkey's servers.

faqs.question: Do I need a license to use the Form Library?
faqs.answer: No, the Form Library is MIT-licensed and free to use without a paid license.

faqs.question: What does the Basic plan include?
faqs.answer: The Basic plan is a one-time developer license that adds the drag-and-drop Survey Creator on top of the free Form Library.

faqs.question: Is SurveyJS open source?
faqs.answer: The core Form Library, PDF Generator rendering, and Dashboard rendering components are open source under the MIT license; the visual Survey Creator builder is commercially licensed.

faqs.question: Which frameworks does SurveyJS support?
faqs.answer: SurveyJS supports React, Angular, Vue, jQuery, and vanilla JavaScript.

faqs.question: Does SurveyJS host my survey data?
faqs.answer: No, SurveyJS runs inside your own application, so response data is stored on your own servers rather than a third-party host.

faqs.question: What is SurveyJS's refund policy?
faqs.answer: SurveyJS offers a 90-day unconditional money-back guarantee on its paid developer licenses.

use_cases.title: Full data ownership requirements

features: Export to Excel, CSV, and PDF

enrichment.short_description: SurveyMonkey is a hosted online survey and feedback platform that lets individuals, teams, and enterprises create, distribute, and analyze surveys without writing code.
enrichment.seo_title: SurveyMonkey: Online Survey and Feedback Software
enrichment.seo_meta_description: SurveyMonkey is a hosted survey platform for creating, distributing, and analyzing surveys. Free plan available; Team plans start at $30/user/month.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: SurveyMonkey is a hosted, no-code online survey and feedback platform founded in 1999 by Ryan and Chris Finley, headquartered in San Mateo, California. Unlike developer-focused libraries such as SurveyJS, SurveyMonkey is a complete SaaS product where surveys are created, hosted, distributed, and analyzed entirely on SurveyMonkey's own servers, targeting non-technical users including market researchers, HR teams, and small businesses. The company briefly rebranded to Momentive Global from 2021 to 2023 to reflect a broader enterprise experience-management product suite, then reverted to the SurveyMonkey name in 2023 after being acquired by an investor group led by Symphony Technology Group. SurveyMonkey offers a free Basic plan with limited features, and paid Team plans starting with Team Advantage at around 30 US dollars per user per month billed annually with a three-user minimum, which includes unlimited surveys and up to 50,000 responses per year. The Team Premier plan, around 75 US dollars per user per month, adds white-label branding, advanced logic, and multilingual surveys. A custom-priced Enterprise plan with a five-user minimum adds SSO, enhanced security, and dedicated support. Responses beyond plan limits are billed per response.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is SurveyMonkey
paragraphs: SurveyMonkey is a hosted online survey platform that lets anyone create, distribute, and analyze surveys through a web-based drag-and-drop builder, with no coding required. Founded in 1999 by Ryan and Chris Finley, it is one of the longest-running names in online survey software.

The company briefly operated as Momentive Global from 2021 to 2023 to reflect a broader enterprise experience-management product line, before reverting to the SurveyMonkey brand in 2023 following an acquisition by an investor group led by Symphony Technology Group. It remains headquartered in San Mateo, California.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: SurveyMonkey provides a drag-and-drop survey builder, a library of over 300 templates, skip logic and branching, and AI-assisted survey creation and response analysis. Higher tiers add white-label branding, advanced logic, image click maps, and multilingual survey support.

The platform integrates with tools such as Salesforce, HubSpot, and Slack, and Enterprise customers get single sign-on (SSO), custom response limits, and data center options for compliance-sensitive organizations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: SurveyMonkey offers a free Basic plan with limited surveys and responses, functioning as both an entry point and a trial. Paid Team plans require a minimum of three user seats.

Team Advantage starts around 30 US dollars per user per month when billed annually and includes unlimited surveys with up to 50,000 responses per year. Team Premier, around 75 US dollars per user per month, adds white-label branding and multilingual surveys. Enterprise pricing is custom, with a five-user minimum, and adds SSO and enhanced security controls.

features.title: Drag-and-drop survey builder
features.description: No-code interface for building surveys from scratch or from a template library.

features.title: 300+ survey templates
features.description: Pre-built templates covering market research, HR, education, and customer feedback use cases.

features.title: AI-assisted survey creation and analysis

features.title: Skip logic and branching

features.title: Enterprise security and SSO

features.title: Integrations
features.description: Connects with Salesforce, HubSpot, Slack, and other business tools.

features.title: Response analytics and reporting
features.description: Built-in charts, filters, and export options for analyzing survey results.

Large template and question library
Strong brand recognition and long operating history since 1999
Enterprise-grade security options including SSO
cons: Response and survey limits on lower tiers restrict larger use cases
Costs rise quickly for teams needing advanced logic or branding
Extra responses beyond plan limits incur per-response fees
Team plans require a minimum of three paid seats
Advanced features are locked behind higher-priced or custom Enterprise plans

faqs.question: Is SurveyMonkey free?
faqs.answer: SurveyMonkey offers a free Basic plan with limited surveys and responses, though paid Team and Enterprise plans unlock higher limits and advanced features.

faqs.question: What is the difference between SurveyMonkey and SurveyJS?
faqs.answer: SurveyMonkey is a fully hosted SaaS platform where surveys and data live on SurveyMonkey's own servers, while SurveyJS is an open-source developer library embedded and self-hosted inside a company's own application.

faqs.question: How much does SurveyMonkey cost per month?
faqs.answer: Team Advantage starts around 30 US dollars per user per month billed annually, and Team Premier starts around 75 US dollars per user per month, both with a three-user minimum.

faqs.question: What happened to Momentive?
faqs.answer: SurveyMonkey rebranded to Momentive Global in 2021, then reverted to the SurveyMonkey name in 2023 after being acquired by an investor group led by Symphony Technology Group.

faqs.question: Does SurveyMonkey have a minimum seat requirement?
faqs.answer: Yes, Team plans require a minimum of three users and Enterprise plans require a minimum of five users.

faqs.question: Can I export survey data from SurveyMonkey?
faqs.answer: Yes, SurveyMonkey supports exporting response data and analytics for further analysis.

faqs.question: Does SurveyMonkey offer enterprise security features?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Enterprise plan includes SSO, enhanced security controls, and data center options.

faqs.question: What is the response limit on paid plans?
faqs.answer: Team Advantage includes up to 50,000 responses per year and Team Premier includes up to 100,000 responses per year, with extra responses billed per response.

use_cases.title: Customer feedback and market research
use_cases.description: Market researchers use SurveyMonkey to design and distribute surveys and analyze customer sentiment.
use_cases.audience: Market researchers and customer insights teams

use_cases.title: Employee engagement surveys
use_cases.description: HR teams run recurring employee engagement and satisfaction surveys using SurveyMonkey's templates.

use_cases.title: Small business customer satisfaction tracking
use_cases.description: Small businesses collect customer satisfaction data without needing developer resources.
use_cases.audience: Small business owners and marketing teams

features: Premium themes and shared brand assets
Advanced logic and image click maps

enrichment.short_description: SvelteKit is a free, open-source web application framework built on top of Svelte that adds file-based routing, server-side rendering, and full-stack app infrastructure.
enrichment.seo_title: SvelteKit: Open-Source Full-Stack Framework for Svelte
enrichment.seo_meta_description: SvelteKit is a free, open-source framework built on Svelte with routing, SSR, and deployment adapters. MIT-licensed and free for any project.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: SvelteKit is a free, open-source web application framework built on top of the Svelte component framework, adding file-based routing, server-side rendering, static site generation, data loading, and deployment infrastructure that Svelte alone does not provide. Svelte itself was created by Rich Harris and first released in 2016, while SvelteKit was announced in October 2020, entered beta in March 2021, and reached its 1.0 stable release in December 2022. SvelteKit occupies a role comparable to Next.js for React or Nuxt for Vue, letting developers build full applications rather than just individual UI components. It is powered by Vite for fast development and hot module replacement, supports multiple rendering modes (server-side rendering, client-side rendering, and static prerendering), and ships with adapters for deploying to platforms such as Vercel, Netlify, Cloudflare, and Node servers. Rich Harris now works on Svelte and SvelteKit full-time as an employee of Vercel, though governance of the open-source project remains independent of any single company. SvelteKit is released under the MIT license and is entirely free to use for personal or commercial projects, with no paid tier, subscription, or usage limits imposed by the project itself.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is SvelteKit
paragraphs: SvelteKit is a free, open-source framework for building full web applications using Svelte, adding the routing, rendering, and deployment infrastructure that the Svelte component library alone does not include. It plays a role similar to Next.js for React or Nuxt for Vue.

Svelte was created by Rich Harris and first released in 2016. SvelteKit itself was announced in October 2020, entered beta in March 2021, and reached its 1.0 stable release in December 2022. Rich Harris now works on Svelte full-time as an employee of Vercel, while governance of the open-source project remains independent.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: SvelteKit provides file-based routing that maps folder structure directly to application routes, along with built-in data-loading functions and form actions for handling server-side logic. It supports server-side rendering, static prerendering, and client-side rendering, often within the same application.

Development is powered by Vite, giving instant hot module replacement and fast build times. Deployment is handled through adapters for platforms including Vercel, Netlify, Cloudflare, and traditional Node servers, and the framework includes built-in image optimization and full TypeScript support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: SvelteKit is free and open source under the MIT license, with no cost to use for personal or commercial projects and no usage limits imposed by the framework itself.
Any hosting costs come from the deployment platform chosen (such as Vercel or Netlify), not from SvelteKit, which does not sell a paid tier or subscription.

features.title: File-based routing
features.description: Maps folder and file structure directly to application routes automatically.

features.title: Multiple rendering modes
features.description: Supports server-side rendering, client-side rendering, and static prerendering in one app.

features.title: Built-in data loading
features.description: Load functions and form actions handle server-side data fetching and mutations.

features.title: Vite-powered dev server
features.description: Instant hot module replacement and fast builds powered by Vite.

features.title: Deployment adapters
features.description: Ships with adapters for Vercel, Netlify, Cloudflare, and Node-based hosting.

features.title: Built-in image optimization
features.description: Optimizes images automatically as part of the build pipeline.

features.title: TypeScript support
features.description: First-class TypeScript support out of the box.

features.title: Offline and service worker support
features.description: Enables offline capability through built-in service worker integration.

pros: Fast runtime performance due to Svelte's compiler-based approach
Flexible rendering modes combined in a single framework
Strong developer experience with Vite-powered hot module replacement
Free and open source under the MIT license
Actively developed with dedicated maintainer support from Vercel
cons: Smaller plugin and library ecosystem than React or Vue-based frameworks
Fewer job listings and enterprise adoption than React-based alternatives
Breaking changes have occurred between major Svelte versions
Finding developers experienced specifically in Svelte and SvelteKit can be harder
Some third-party tools and integrations prioritize React or Vue first

faqs.question: Is SvelteKit free?
faqs.answer: Yes, SvelteKit is free and open source under the MIT license with no usage limits or paid tier.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Svelte and SvelteKit?
faqs.answer: Svelte is a component-level UI framework, while SvelteKit is the full application framework built on top of it, adding routing, rendering, and deployment infrastructure.

faqs.question: Who created SvelteKit?
faqs.answer: SvelteKit was built by the Svelte core team led by Rich Harris, the original creator of Svelte.

faqs.question: Is SvelteKit production-ready?
faqs.answer: Yes, SvelteKit reached its stable 1.0 release in December 2022 and is used in production by many companies.

faqs.question: Can I deploy SvelteKit to Vercel or Netlify?
faqs.answer: Yes, SvelteKit ships with official adapters for Vercel, Netlify, Cloudflare, and Node-based hosting.

faqs.question: Does SvelteKit support server-side rendering?
faqs.answer: Yes, SvelteKit supports server-side rendering, static prerendering, and client-side rendering within the same application.

faqs.question: What license is SvelteKit under?
faqs.answer: SvelteKit is released under the MIT license.

faqs.question: How is SvelteKit different from Next.js?
faqs.answer: SvelteKit is built on the Svelte compiler rather than React, which generally results in smaller bundle sizes, while offering a comparable feature set of routing, SSR, and data loading.

use_cases.title: Performant SSR and SSG web apps
use_cases.description: Frontend developers build server-rendered or statically generated sites that need fast load times.

use_cases.title: Lightweight full-stack startup apps
use_cases.description: Startups choose SvelteKit as a compact full-stack framework for shipping products quickly.

use_cases.title: Migrating from Svelte components to a full app
use_cases.description: Teams already using Svelte components adopt SvelteKit to add routing and server rendering.
use_cases.audience: Teams already invested in the Svelte ecosystem

features: Open source under the MIT license
Free for personal and commercial projects
No usage limits from the framework itself
Deployable to any adapter-supported hosting platform

enrichment.short_description: Svix is a webhooks-as-a-service platform that lets companies send reliable, secure webhooks to their customers without building and maintaining webhook infrastructure in-house.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Svix is a webhooks-as-a-service platform with automatic retries, message transformations, and enterprise SLAs. Free tier available; paid from $490/month.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Svix is a webhooks-as-a-service platform that lets companies add reliable, secure outbound webhooks to their own product without building and maintaining that infrastructure themselves. Founded in 2021 by Tom Hacohen, Svix went through Y Combinator's W21 batch and has raised funding from investors including Andreessen Horowitz (a16z) and Aleph, along with founders and CTOs from companies like GitHub, PagerDuty, Segment, and Lookout. The company operates with a small, distributed team of roughly ten to twelve people. Svix provides managed webhook sending with automatic retries and exponential backoff, an embeddable customer-facing webhook management UI, message transformations and filtering, HMAC payload signing, and typed event schemas. It offers both a free tier, with 200 messages per second and 50,000 included messages per month on a 99.9 percent uptime SLA, and paid Professional plans starting around 490 US dollars per month with higher throughput, longer payload retention, and a 99.99 percent uptime SLA. An Enterprise tier offers custom pricing with a 99.999 percent uptime SLA, on-premises deployment, and SSO. Svix also offers an open-source, self-hostable version of its webhook sending service for teams that want full control over their infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Svix
paragraphs: Svix is a webhooks-as-a-service platform that handles the sending, retrying, and monitoring of outbound webhooks on behalf of other companies, so those companies do not need to build and maintain that infrastructure themselves.

Founded in 2021 by Tom Hacohen and backed by Y Combinator (W21), Andreessen Horowitz, and Aleph, Svix serves companies of all sizes that need to notify their own customers of events happening inside their product in real time.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Svix manages webhook delivery with automatic retries and exponential backoff, HMAC signing for payload verification, and message transformations and filtering so customers only receive the events they care about. It also provides an embeddable webhook management UI that companies can drop directly into their own product for their customers.
For larger customers, Svix offers typed event schemas, OpenTelemetry log streaming, and enterprise controls such as OIDC and SAML SSO, static source IPs, and on-premises deployment options.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Svix's Free plan includes 200 messages per second, 50,000 included messages per month, 30-day payload retention, and a 99.9 percent uptime SLA, with no credit card required.

The Professional plan starts around 490 US dollars per month and adds higher throughput, 90-day retention, white-label headers, static source IPs, and a 99.99 percent uptime SLA. Enterprise pricing is custom and adds a 99.999 percent uptime SLA, on-premises deployment, and dedicated account support.

features.title: Managed webhook delivery
features.description: Handles sending webhooks to customer endpoints without requiring in-house infrastructure.

features.title: Automatic retries with backoff
features.description: Retries failed webhook deliveries automatically using exponential backoff.

features.title: Embeddable management UI
features.description: A pre-built webhook management interface companies can embed for their own customers.

features.title: Message transformations and filtering
features.description: Lets customers filter and transform events before they are delivered.

features.title: HMAC payload signing
features.description: Signs webhook payloads so receivers can verify authenticity.

features.title: Typed event schemas
features.description: Defines structured event catalogs for consistent webhook payloads.

features.title: OpenTelemetry log streaming
features.description: Streams delivery logs for observability on Enterprise plans.

features.title: Open-source self-hosted option
features.description: Offers an open-source version of the webhook sending service for self-hosting.

pros: Removes the need to build webhook infrastructure in-house
Generous free tier suitable for smaller-scale use
Backed by Y Combinator and well-known venture investors
Open-source self-hosted option available for full control
High uptime SLAs on paid tiers for reliability-sensitive use cases
cons: Professional tier pricing is a significant jump from the free tier
Small team size compared to larger infrastructure vendors
Enterprise features like SSO and on-premises deployment require custom sales-negotiated pricing
Niche product category with fewer public case studies than mainstream API tools
Headquarters and public company details are less documented than larger competitors

faqs.question: Is Svix free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Svix offers a free tier with 200 messages per second and 50,000 included messages per month, with no credit card required.

faqs.question: What does Svix cost at scale?
faqs.answer: The Professional plan starts around 490 US dollars per month, with custom Enterprise pricing available for larger-scale or higher-SLA needs.

faqs.question: Is Svix open source?
faqs.answer: Svix offers an open-source, self-hostable version of its webhook sending service in addition to its managed cloud offering.

faqs.question: Who founded Svix?
faqs.answer: Svix was founded in 2021 by Tom Hacohen and went through Y Combinator's W21 batch.

faqs.question: What is webhooks-as-a-service?
faqs.answer: Webhooks-as-a-service is a managed platform that handles sending, retrying, and monitoring outbound webhooks on behalf of another company's product.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Svix?
faqs.answer: Yes, Svix provides an open-source version that can be self-hosted for teams that want full infrastructure control.

faqs.question: Does Svix support retries for failed webhooks?
faqs.answer: Yes, Svix automatically retries failed webhook deliveries using exponential backoff.

faqs.question: What SLA does Svix guarantee?
faqs.answer: The Free plan includes a 99.9 percent uptime SLA, Professional includes 99.99 percent, and Enterprise includes 99.999 percent.

use_cases.title: Adding webhooks to a SaaS product
use_cases.description: SaaS companies use Svix to let their own customers subscribe to real-time event notifications.

use_cases.title: Reliable event delivery at scale
use_cases.description: Platforms use Svix to ensure event delivery with retries, monitoring, and alerting built in.

use_cases.title: High-SLA or on-premises webhook needs
use_cases.description: Enterprises with strict compliance or uptime requirements use Svix's Enterprise plan for on-premises or high-SLA delivery.

features: White-label headers and static source IPs

enrichment.short_description: Swapcard is a revenue-first event management platform that helps organizers run in-person, virtual, and hybrid events with AI-powered attendee matchmaking and exhibitor tools.
enrichment.seo_title: Swapcard: AI-Powered Event Management and Networking Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Swapcard is an event management platform with AI matchmaking, exhibitor tools, and hybrid event support. Plans start around $610/year per license.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Swapcard is a revenue-first event management platform built for organizers running in-person, virtual, and hybrid B2B events, conferences, and trade shows. It was founded in 2013 in Paris, France by Baptiste Boulard, Godefroy des Francs, and Damien Courbon, who set out to fix fragmented event technology by unifying operations and attendee engagement into one shared data layer. The company is headquartered in Paris and, according to public company data, employs between roughly 201 and 500 people. Over a twelve-month period, Swapcard has reported supporting more than 4,000 events across over 92 countries, engaging more than 7 million attendees and 120,000 exhibitors. The platform's core differentiator is AI-powered matchmaking that connects attendees and exhibitors based on shared interests, alongside an event app, virtual and hybrid streaming, exhibitor lead capture and qualification, real-time analytics, and revenue reporting for organizers. Swapcard uses annual license-based pricing that scales with attendee volume rather than per-event fees, starting around 610 US dollars per year per license for its Starter tier covering up to 1,000 attendees per year, with Professional and Enterprise tiers requiring a custom quote. The platform holds SOC 2, ISO 27001, and GDPR compliance certifications and offers discounted pricing for qualifying nonprofits.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Swapcard
paragraphs: Swapcard is a revenue-first event management platform that helps organizers run in-person, virtual, and hybrid B2B events, conferences, and trade shows from a single system. It was founded in 2013 in Paris by Baptiste Boulard, Godefroy des Francs, and Damien Courbon to solve the fragmented systems many large B2B event organizers relied on.

The platform is headquartered in Paris, France, and, according to public company data, employs between roughly 201 and 500 people. Swapcard has reported supporting thousands of events across dozens of countries, engaging millions of attendees and exhibitors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Swapcard's core differentiator is AI-powered matchmaking that connects attendees and exhibitors based on shared interests and goals, supported by a branded event app for web and mobile. The platform supports in-person, virtual, and hybrid formats, including live streaming for remote attendees.

For exhibitors and sponsors, Swapcard provides lead capture and qualification tools, booth analytics, and sponsorship management, while organizers get real-time attendance and revenue reporting alongside CRM and marketing integrations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Swapcard uses an annual license-based pricing model tied to attendee volume rather than per-event fees. The Starter tier begins around 610 US dollars per year per license and covers up to 1,000 attendees per year, with a free trial available.

The Professional tier covers up to 10,000 attendees per year and requires a sales demo for a custom quote, while the Enterprise tier supports unlimited attendees with a full security review and custom contract terms. Optional add-ons for exhibitor services and support tiers can increase total cost, and discounted pricing is available for qualifying nonprofits.

features.title: AI-powered attendee matchmaking
features.description: Connects attendees and exhibitors based on shared interests and goals to drive networking outcomes.

features.title: Branded event app
features.description: A customizable web and mobile app for attendees to navigate agendas, network, and engage during events.

features.title: Exhibitor lead capture and qualification
features.description: Tools for exhibitors to scan, capture, and qualify leads at booths and virtual sessions.

features.title: Hybrid and virtual event support
features.description: Live streaming and virtual engagement tools alongside in-person event features.

features.title: Real-time analytics and revenue reporting
features.description: Dashboards that track attendance, engagement, and revenue for organizers.

features.title: Sponsorship and exhibitor management
features.description: Tools to manage sponsor packages, booth assignments, and exhibitor communications.

features.title: One-to-one meeting scheduling
features.description: Lets attendees and exhibitors book meetings directly within the event app.

features.title: CRM and marketing integrations
features.description: Connects event data with CRM and marketing platforms for follow-up and attribution.

pros: Strong AI-driven matchmaking built specifically for B2B networking events
Supports in-person, virtual, and hybrid event formats in one platform
Proven at scale across thousands of events and millions of attendees
Robust security and compliance certifications including SOC 2 and ISO 27001
Revenue-focused reporting tailored to event organizer business goals
cons: Pricing for Professional and Enterprise tiers requires a sales call rather than being published
Annual subscription model may not suit organizers running only a single small event
Optional add-ons for exhibitor services and support can increase total cost
Platform depth can create a learning curve for organizers new to the system
Attendee-volume-based pricing can become costly for very large events

faqs.question: How much does Swapcard cost?
faqs.answer: Swapcard's Starter tier begins around 610 US dollars per year per license for up to 1,000 attendees per year, while Professional and Enterprise tiers require a custom quote from sales.

faqs.question: Who founded Swapcard?
faqs.answer: Swapcard was founded in 2013 in Paris, France, by Baptiste Boulard, Godefroy des Francs, and Damien Courbon.

faqs.question: Does Swapcard support hybrid events?
faqs.answer: Yes, Swapcard supports in-person, virtual, and hybrid event formats, including live streaming for remote attendees.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial for Swapcard?
faqs.answer: Yes, a free trial is available for the Starter tier.

faqs.question: What is included in the Starter plan?
faqs.answer: The Starter plan covers up to 1,000 attendees per year with core platform access and compliance certifications including SOC 2, ISO 27001, and GDPR.

faqs.question: How does Swapcard's AI matchmaking work?
faqs.answer: Swapcard's AI matches attendees and exhibitors based on shared interests and goals to help them find relevant connections during an event.

faqs.question: Does Swapcard offer nonprofit discounts?
faqs.answer: Yes, qualifying nonprofits and NGOs may be eligible for discounted pricing through application.

faqs.question: What security certifications does Swapcard have?
faqs.answer: Swapcard holds SOC 2, ISO 27001, and GDPR compliance certifications.

use_cases.title: B2B conference networking
use_cases.description: Conference organizers use Swapcard's AI matchmaking to help attendees find relevant connections and sessions.

use_cases.title: Trade show exhibitor management
use_cases.description: Trade show organizers manage exhibitor booths, lead capture, and sponsorship packages through Swapcard.

use_cases.title: Hybrid association events
use_cases.description: Associations run hybrid events that combine in-person attendance with virtual streaming for remote members.
